text: During port insertion, the surgeon ruptures a large para-ovarian cyst measuring approximately twenty to twenty-five centimeters in size.
The surgeon performs a direct trocar entry into the large ovarian cyst, causing it to debulk as the cyst contents are released.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and scissors to make an incision over the anti-macentric or anti-hylar border of the ovary, creating a fold of tissue to open up the ovary.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery at its lowest setting to minimize damage to the ovarian cortex and preserve the ovarian reserve during the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon opens the ovary into two distinct folds of tissue, preparing for a complete cystectomy by enhancing visibility and access to the cyst.

The surgeon initiates the cystectomy by applying gentle traction and counter traction using needle holders. One needle holder grips the ovarian cortex while the other holds the cyst wall, facilitating meticulous dissection.
During the cystectomy, the surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to manage tiny capillaries and venous oozers, ensuring minimal bleeding while dissecting through the correct tissue plane.
The surgeon performs the cystectomy without prior infiltration of vasopressin, carefully dissecting the cyst wall from the ovarian tissue while minimizing bleeding.
The surgical team utilizes three assistant ports to facilitate the ovarian cystectomy procedure, providing access and maneuverability for instruments during the operation.
The surgeon encounters increased bleeding while dissecting near the hilar region of the cyst wall during the ovarian cystectomy.
The surgeon has fully opened the bed of the ovarian cyst, revealing a clean surgical field with minimal bleeding due to meticulous dissection, reducing the need for additional cautery.
The surgeon begins dissection of the large para ovarian cyst, which was ruptured earlier in the surgery. The collapsed wall of the para ovarian cyst is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to apply continuous, steady traction on the para ovarian cyst while employing bipolars or plain graspers to provide gentle counter traction. This technique facilitates the smooth sliding of mesosalpingial tissue or fascia over the cyst during the cystectomy.
The completed para ovarian cystectomy is shown, with the surgical field displaying the area where the cyst was removed.
The surgeon identifies the ovarian cyst located in the left gutter and the para-ovarian cyst situated in the right gutter, highlighting their positions for better visualization.
The surgeon introduces an endobag through the ten mm lateral port and unfolds it towards the pelvis for the retrieval of large cystectomy specimens.

The surgeon gently places the cystectomy specimen into the endobag, which is positioned through the ten mm lateral port. The specimen is handled carefully due to a suspected borderline component in one of the cysts.

The surgeon employs the ribbonizing technique on a large ovarian cyst, using two needle holders to grasp the ends of the cyst while scissors cut through the center, creating elongated ribbons for easier retrieval through the abdominal wall.
The surgeon utilizes three ports during the cystectomy procedure: two lateral ports provide traction, while scissors inserted through the central port cut the ovarian cyst into elongated ribbons.

The surgeon begins the para-ovarian cystectomy, demonstrating the ribbonizing technique inside the endobag. Scissors are used to cut the cyst into elongated ribbons, facilitating easier retrieval through the abdominal wall.
The cyst specimen is transformed into a single long stripe within the endobag, demonstrating the effectiveness of the ribbonizing technique in compressing the specimen for easier retrieval.
The surgeon uses a needle holder or alleys forceps to remove the cystectomy specimen from the endobag through the port, revealing the specimen as a long strip of tissue.
The surgeon extracts the elongated stripe of ovarian cyst tissue from the abdomen, demonstrating the effectiveness of the ribbonizing technique for specimen removal.
The surgeon places two or three sutures over the surface of the ovary to keep the long ovarian cyst crumpled inside the pouch of Douglas.
